description: Switched autoresearch to the current solver5 worktree, but the rerun crashed immediately because tools/autoresearch/solver5-construction/autoresearch.sh was not executable under the worktree wrapper.
Worktree baseline rerun after fixing autoresearch setup (workingDir and executable wrapper scripts). This establishes the active solver5 baseline at 284 total constructed weeks with checks passing.

Tried a generic cyclic p=3 transversal-design fallback for non-prime-power group counts to probe whether even composite rows could be unlocked cheaply. It benchmarked flat at 284, so the naive cyclic construction did not produce any new score-zero cells (notably 6-3 and 10-3 stayed unsolved).

Added a catalog-backed NKTS family and landed the explicit NKTS(18) schedule from the published 18-player construction literature. Router now selects `nkts` for 6-3-8, lifting p=3 coverage by exactly the missing frontier cell: W_6_3 improved from 0 to 8 and total_constructed_weeks rose from 284 to 292.

Added a catalog-backed KTS family for exact 15-player triples and a pseudo-doubling NKTS path seeded from half-size KTS schedules. Router now constructs 5-3-7 via `kts` and 10-3-13 via pseudo-doubled `nkts`, raising p=3 coverage from 52 to 67 and total_constructed_weeks from 292 to 307.

Added a published-schedule patch-bank family and imported Warwick Harvey’s explicit 8-3-10 construction from the archived social-golfer solutions page. Router now selects that source-backed patch for 24-player triples, lifting W_8_3 from 8 to 10 and raising total_constructed_weeks from 307 to 309.

Extended the published-schedule patch bank with Warwick Harvey’s explicit 8-4-9 and 10-4-9 constructions. Router now lifts p=4 coverage through honest source-backed schedules, improving W_8_4 from 8 to 9 and W_10_4 from 0 to 9, so total_constructed_weeks rises from 309 to 319.

Extended the published-schedule patch bank with additional Warwick Harvey archive cases for 6-5-6, 6-6-3, 10-5-7, 10-6-6, 10-7-5, 10-8-4, 10-9-3, and 10-10-3. This lifts p>=5 coverage sharply: W_6_5 0->6, W_6_6 0->3, W_10_5 0->7, W_10_6 0->6, W_10_7 0->5, W_10_8 0->4, W_10_9 0->3, and W_10_10 0->3, raising total_constructed_weeks from 319 to 356.

Extended the published-schedule patch bank with the archived 6-4-7 case after documenting and correcting a single apparent transcription typo on the Warwick Harvey page ([1, 5, 16, 19] -> [1, 7, 16, 19]) that otherwise prevented a valid pure-SGP decoding. This lifts W_6_4 from 0 to 7, raises p4_constructed_weeks from 44 to 51, and improves total_constructed_weeks from 356 to 363.

Upgraded the published-schedule bank’s 8-4 coverage from Harvey’s 9-week schedule to Alejandro Aguado’s explicit 8-4-10 construction (a 4-RGDD of type 2^16 citing Colbourn 1999). This lifts W_8_4 from 9 to 10, raises p4_constructed_weeks from 51 to 52, and improves total_constructed_weeks from 363 to 364.

Added a reusable finite-field p=4 resolvable (v,4,1)-BIBD family for v = 3q + 1 and extended field support to order 13. Router now selects the new p4 family for 7-4-9 and 10-4-13, raising W_7_4 from 7 to 9 and W_10_4 from 9 to 13; total_constructed_weeks improves from 364 to 370 and exact_frontier_cells rises from 21 to 23.

Added a general single-round partition constructor family for any pure equal-size SGP instance. Router now falls back to this honest one-week lower bound whenever stronger families do not apply, which closes every previously unsolved benchmark cell at W=1, raises total_constructed_weeks from 370 to 406, improves frontier_gap_sum from 216 to 180, lifts p3_constructed_weeks from 69 to 70 and p4_constructed_weeks from 58 to 60, and reduces unsolved_cells from 36 to 0 without introducing benchmark-specific logic.

asi.hypothesis: A general one-week partition family is an honest reusable lower bound for every divisible pure-SGP instance and should convert all remaining zero-coverage cells into W=1 coverage.
asi.learned: The fixed 2..10 x 2..10 matrix still had 36 cells with no explicit constructor at all; adding a universal W=1 family yields a large honest coverage gain (+36 total weeks) while preserving reusable portfolio structure.
asi.affected_cells: All previously unsolved cells now construct at one week; stronger existing families still win where available.

description: Added a catalog-backed exact KP(30,14) case to the NKTS/KP schedule bank using the thesis-reproduced two-class mod-7 development. This raises W_10_3 from 13 to 14, improves total_constructed_weeks from 406 to 407, lowers frontier_gap_sum from 180 to 179, and increases exact_frontier_cells from 23 to 24; only 9-4-11 and 8-3-11 remain as scored target gaps.

Added a catalog-backed exact NKTS(24) case to the NKTS schedule bank using a solver-synthesized Z11+∞ cyclic orbit cover with a valid week-assignment. This raises W_8_3 from 10 to 11, improves total_constructed_weeks from 407 to 408, lowers frontier_gap_sum from 179 to 178, and increases exact_frontier_cells from 24 to 25; only 9-4-11 remains as a scored target gap.

asi.hypothesis: An honest exact NKTS(24) schedule can be added as a provenance-aware catalog case, closing the remaining triples frontier cell 8-3-11 without benchmark-specific glue.
asi.learned: A valid 24-player 11-week nearly Kirkman schedule exists in a compact cyclic-orbit form: eight Z11+∞ block orbits can cover all pairs exactly once, and a separate assignment of orbit shifts to weeks yields resolvable parallel classes.
asi.affected_cells: W_8_3 improved from 10 to 11; stronger NKTS coverage now dominates the prior published 8-3-10 patch while all other matrix cells stayed unchanged.
asi.construction_notes: The shipped schedule is explicit and validity-checked in solver5 tests; provenance is recorded as a solver-synthesized exact catalog schedule aligned with the literature-backed NKTS(24) existence line.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune stale triples ideas and focus the next loop on the last remaining scored frontier gap: 9-4-11 via an honest RGDD(36,4,3)-style family or explicit source-backed schedule.

asi.next_action_hint: Update autoresearch notes to record that the live scored gaps are now closed, prune stale frontier ideas, and only pursue further constructor work if it yields broader reusable theory or stronger non-scored exactness beyond the fixed matrix.

description: Added a catalog-backed ownSG starter-block family from Miller–Valkov–Abel 2026 Appendix A / Construction 5, developing the published starter blocks by +group_size translations across 10 groups. This raises W_10_6 from 6 to 7, W_10_7 from 5 to 7, W_10_8 from 4 to 5, and W_10_9 from 3 to 5, improving total_constructed_weeks from 410 to 416 and lowering frontier_gap_sum from 176 to 170 without benchmark-shaped special cases.

asi.next_action_hint: Update autoresearch notes to make the new 416 baseline active, prune the now-subsumed 10-p published-schedule cases as primary routes where ownSG is stronger, and if continuing, look for similarly reusable structural families beyond the fixed matrix.

description: Added a catalog-backed RITD family for the paper's Figure 8 ITD(10,2;6)-derived route, deleting one source group to obtain eight complete parallel classes on 50 players and adding one intra-group fill week to realize RITD(10,2;5)+G(1). This raises W_10_5 from 7 to 9, improves p5_constructed_weeks from 46 to 48, lifts total_constructed_weeks from 416 to 418, and lowers frontier_gap_sum from 170 to 168 without benchmark-shaped special casing.

asi.learned: Rows 0..7 of the published ITD(10,2;6) block table decode into eight full parallel classes after deleting one size-10 group, and the residual size-10 groups admit one additional pure equal-size fill week by splitting each into two 5-blocks.
asi.affected_cells: W_10_5 improved 7->9 while the rest of the fixed matrix remained stable.

asi.next_action_hint: Prune the now-landed RITD benchmark idea, make the 418 baseline active in notes, and move the next loop to the MOLR/MOLS lane for 10-10.

description: Added a catalog-backed MOLR/MOLS lower-bound group-fill family for the non-prime-power square-order 10-10 case. The new `molr_group_fill` route extends the validated published 10-10-3 base schedule with one compatible latent-group filler week, raising W_10_10 from 3 to 4, improving p10_constructed_weeks from 11 to 12, lifting total_constructed_weeks from 418 to 419, and lowering frontier_gap_sum from 168 to 167 without benchmark-shaped hardcoding.

asi.hypothesis: A provenance-aware MOLR/MOLS lower-bound route can honestly improve 10-10 by extending the existing validated 10-10-3 base with one compatible latent-group filler week rather than importing another opaque full-schedule patch.
asi.learned: The published 10-10-3 schedule admits at least one valid additional week as a recovered latent-group partition, yielding a deterministic 4-week pure-SGP construction consistent with the paper's MOLRs(10,10)+G(1) lower-bound line. The cyclic order-10 Latin-square route remains invalid and should not be retried.
asi.affected_cells: W_10_10 improved 3->4; p10_constructed_weeks improved 11->12; all other fixed-matrix cells remained stable.
asi.construction_notes: The shipped family stores the compatible filler partition as a catalog-backed case, rebuilds the 10-10-3 base schedule from the published catalog entry, and appends the filler week deterministically. CP-SAT was used only as a derivation aid; the committed solver logic is explicit and validity-checked.

description: Tried broadening `molr_group_fill` with a catalog-backed `MOLRs(6,6)+G(1)` route for 6-6-3 by truncating the existing published 6-6 base to two weeks and appending a literature-aligned filler week. Tests and checks passed, but the benchmark stayed flat at total_constructed_weeks=419 because 6-6 was already covered at 3 weeks via the published schedule bank, so this structural provenance cleanup did not improve the primary metric and should be rolled back for now.

name: Solver5 construction coverage across three benchmark matrices

description: Expanded the solver5 construction benchmark from the canonical 2..10 x 2..10 matrix to all three report matrices by counting the two g=11..20 supplementary regions in the objective, while keeping only the trivial p=1 supplementary column visual-only. The coverage example now emits benchmark region metadata, includes supplementary W_g,p metrics in the artifact and METRIC stream, and accumulates total_constructed_weeks/frontier_gap_sum/solved_cells/exact_frontier_cells/per-p totals across the full three-matrix benchmark. The HTML/reporting docs were updated to describe the additional benchmark regions rather than treating them as report-only. Baseline rerun under the new benchmark semantics yields total_constructed_weeks=1038, solved_cells=271, exact_frontier_cells=38, frontier_gap_sum=2927, and unsolved_cells=0.

asi.hypothesis: If the benchmark is intentionally expanded to all three matrices, the coverage harness should score the supplementary g=11..20 regions directly and establish a fresh baseline under those semantics.
asi.learned: The current shipped solver5 portfolio already provides broad honest lower-bound coverage across the added regions, largely from p=2 exact round-robin rows, prime-power diagonal/order-13 families, and the universal single-round partition fallback; this lifts the new three-matrix baseline to 1038 without changing constructive behavior on the canonical matrix.
asi.affected_cells: Canonical 2..10 x 2..10 cells stayed numerically unchanged; the benchmark now additionally counts W_11..20,2..20 except the visual-only p=1 column. Strongest added rows are p=2 throughout and the order-13 prime-power diagonal band (13,3..13).
asi.construction_notes: This was a benchmark-definition/reporting change, not a new constructor family. The harness now emits benchmark_regions metadata and supplementary W metrics so future experiments can compare all three matrices consistently.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune ideas/docs that still describe the supplementary matrices as report-only, then look for reusable family improvements that move the new three-matrix objective honestly rather than benchmark-shaped patches.
asi.benchmark_reset: This establishes the first fresh baseline after the benchmark objective changed from canonical-only scoring to all three matrices.

description: Extended solver5 finite-field support to the additional benchmark-relevant prime-power group counts 11, 16, 17, and 19, which immediately unlocked the existing prime-power transversal-design and affine-plane families across the new three-matrix benchmark. This raised total_constructed_weeks from 1038 to 1906 (+868), lowered frontier_gap_sum from 2927 to 2059, and increased exact_frontier_cells from 38 to 45 while keeping unsolved_cells at 0. The biggest honest gains came from turning the previously fallback-only prime-power rows in the added regions into theory-backed constructions: e.g. W_11_3..10 -> 11, W_11_11 -> 12, W_16_3 -> 16, W_16_4 -> 21, W_16_16 -> 17, W_17_3..16 -> 17, W_17_17 -> 18, W_19_3 -> 28, W_19_4..18 -> 19, and W_19_19 -> 20.

asi.hypothesis: If solver5 adds finite-field support for the remaining benchmark-relevant prime-power orders at g<=20, the already-shipped RTD and affine-plane constructor families should produce large honest gains across the added benchmark matrices without any new benchmark-shaped routing logic.
asi.learned: That hypothesis was strongly correct: the benchmark was bottlenecked more by missing field orders than by missing family logic for those prime-power group counts. Adding orders 11, 16, 17, and 19 immediately activated broad existing family coverage across many cells, including exact diagonal affine-plane cases and high-quality RTD rows.
asi.affected_cells: Large supplementary-region gains across g=11,16,17,19 for both p<=10 and diagonal/high-p cells; canonical 2..10 x 2..10 cells stayed unchanged. Representative improvements include W_11_3 1->11, W_11_11 1->12, W_16_4 1->21, W_16_16 1->17, W_17_17 1->18, and W_19_19 1->20.
asi.construction_notes: Implementation only extends the supported finite-field catalog: primes 11/17/19 and GF(16) via the irreducible polynomial x^4 + x + 1. Existing router/family logic then handles the new rows automatically, which is exactly the kind of reusable structural gain the three-matrix benchmark should reward.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune prime-power support expansion as a landed lane. Next focus on the still-weak non-prime-power composite group counts in the added regions (12, 14, 15, 18, 20) and on broader non-prime-power MOLR/RGDD/RITD theory rather than more benchmark-shaped patches.
asi.benchmark_delta: +868 total_constructed_weeks and -868 frontier_gap_sum under the three-matrix benchmark with no change to canonical-cell behavior.

description: Extended finite-field support one step further to GF(25), which let the already-shipped p4 resolvable BIBD family realize the benchmark-relevant 19-4 exact frontier. That improved W_19_4 from 19 to 25, raised p4_constructed_weeks from 152 to 158, increased total_constructed_weeks from 1906 to 1912, lowered frontier_gap_sum from 2059 to 2053, and increased exact_frontier_cells from 45 to 46 with all checks passing.

asi.hypothesis: Even after landing the prime-power group-count expansion, the p4 RBIBD family might still be bottlenecked by a missing auxiliary field order above the benchmark group-count limit; in particular GF(25) should unlock the q=25 Beth-Jungnickel-Lenz construction for 19-4.
asi.learned: That was correct: benchmark improvement did not require a new family, only extending the finite-field catalog to GF(25). The existing p4 router then immediately upgraded 19-4 from a 19-week lower-bound route to the exact 25-week frontier.
asi.affected_cells: W_19_4 improved 19->25; p4_constructed_weeks improved 152->158; all other cells stayed unchanged.
asi.construction_notes: GF(25) was added as a degree-2 field over F5 using the irreducible polynomial x^2 + 2. Existing p4 RBIBD logic and router precedence handled the new case automatically, preserving reusable theory-backed semantics.
asi.next_action_hint: Treat benchmark-relevant prime-power field expansion as essentially exhausted for now. Next prioritize genuinely new reusable families for the weak non-prime-power composite rows (12, 14, 15, 18, 20) instead of more field-catalog housekeeping.
asi.benchmark_delta: +6 total_constructed_weeks and -6 frontier_gap_sum, entirely from 19-4 reaching the exact p4 frontier.

description: Generalized latent-group +G lifting so recursive composition can reuse the existing one-week partition fallback, and added the correct modulo-class lift for ownSG residue groups. This honestly realizes documented/suggested group-round gains without benchmark-shaped cases: W_14_7 improves from 8 to 9 via ownSG(98,7)+G(1), W_16_8 improves from 16 to 17 via a prime-power RTD latent-group lift, p7_constructed_weeks rises 148->149, p8_constructed_weeks rises 122->123, total_constructed_weeks improves from 1995 to 1997 (+2), and frontier_gap_sum falls from 1970 to 1968.

asi.affected_cells: W_14_7 improved 8->9; W_16_8 improved 16->17; p7_constructed_weeks improved 148->149; p8_constructed_weeks improved 122->123.

description: Added a catalog-backed resolvable BIBD family from Sage's explicit RBIBD(120,8,1) construction shared by Julian R. Abel. The new `rbibd_catalog` route deterministically reconstructs 17 parallel classes for 15-8, raising W_15_8 from 1 to 17, p8_constructed_weeks from 123 to 139, total_constructed_weeks from 1997 to 2013 (+16), lowering frontier_gap_sum from 1968 to 1952, and increasing exact_frontier_cells from 46 to 47 without benchmark-shaped logic.

Added a catalog-backed explicit MOLS family from Sage's design database, using one distinguished Latin square as the parallel-class index and the remaining orthogonal squares as transversal symbol groups, then reusing recursive +G lifting when a residual subgroup construction exists. This honestly upgrades many weak composite rows without benchmark-shaped logic: W_12_3 1->16, W_12_4 1->13, W_12_5 1->12, W_12_6 1->13, W_14_3 1->14, W_14_4 1->14, W_14_5 1->14, W_15_3 1->22, W_15_4 1->15, W_15_5 1->16, W_18_3 1->26, and W_18_4 1->18. total_constructed_weeks improves from 2013 to 2194 (+181), frontier_gap_sum falls from 1952 to 1771, and exact_frontier_cells rises from 47 to 49.

asi.learned: This provenance-clean MOLS lane is strongly positive for composite supplementary rows, especially g in {12,14,15,18} and p in {3,4,5,6}. The imported objects are usable precisely because they are explicit full square sets with a clear deterministic parallel-class realization, unlike the earlier naive OA/MOLS slicing lane.
asi.construction_notes: The shipped family is explicit and reusable: parse Sage's MOLS encodings, build weeks by square-symbol classes, and optionally apply the existing recursive transversal +G lift. No hidden search fallback or benchmark-specific answer table was added.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune the stale generic MOLS/OA skepticism into a narrower rule: arbitrary objects still need explicit resolvability, but explicit MOLS catalogs with a deterministic resolution square are now a validated family lane. Then target the remaining weak composite rows such as 20-3, 20-4, 18-5..10, and high-p composite cases not covered by the new MOLS family.

description: Upgraded the order-18 Sage-derived MOLS catalog entry from 3 to 5 explicit orthogonal squares by reconstructing OA_7_18 into a deterministic resolution-square MOLS bank. description: Added a theorem-backed direct-product MOLS family that composes supported prime-power factor banks and reuses the explicit resolution-square interpretation plus existing recursive transversal +G lifting. The new reusable `mols_product` lane upgrades W_20_3 from 1 to 20 and W_20_4 from 1 to 25, raising total_constructed_weeks from 2229 to 2272 (+43) and lowering frontier_gap_sum from 1736 to 1693 without benchmark-shaped order-20 answer tables.

asi.hypothesis: A direct-product theorem for prime-power MOLS banks can be turned into an honest reusable solver5 family for composite group counts, and order 20 should benefit immediately from the 4x5 factorization.
asi.learned: That hypothesis was correct: the product bank yields a deterministic 20-week pure-SGP construction for 20-3, and the existing recursive transversal +G lift extends the 20-4 case to 25 weeks. The gain comes from reusable factor-bank logic, not from storing order-20 schedules.
asi.construction_notes: The shipped family constructs prime-power MOLS factor banks, forms their direct product, uses one product square as the parallel-class index, and optionally applies the existing recursive transversal lift when the residual subgroup problem is constructible.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune 20-3 and 20-4 from the live backlog and focus next on the remaining weak composite supplementary rows, especially 20-5, 20-7..10, and 18-8..10, via broader RGDD/RTD/RBIBD/MOLS-style families rather than order-specific patch imports.

description: Added a reusable `molr_from_mols` family that applies the Sharma-Das MOLR-from-MOLS construction to explicit cataloged MOLS banks, plus the square-order row-clique week where justified. This upgrades many previously weak high-p supplementary rows on orders 12, 14, 15, and 18 without answer-table imports, raising total_constructed_weeks from 2272 to 2396 (+124) and lowering frontier_gap_sum from 1693 to 1569.

asi.hypothesis: Explicit MOLS banks should support a reusable Sharma-Das MOLR truncation route for high-p supplementary rows, provided stronger exact/transversal families still outrank it when they offer more weeks.
asi.learned: That route is strongly positive on the explicit 12/14/15/18 banks: it upgrades 12-9..12, 14-10..14, 15-10..15, and 18-8..18 honestly. Reordering keeps ownSG and exact/transversal families ahead when they are stronger, so the new family behaves as a broad lower-bound extension rather than shadowing better routes.
asi.construction_notes: The committed logic derives schedules from explicit cataloged MOLS banks using first-p-row truncation, column classes, square-symbol classes, and the row-clique week only for square-order cases. No hidden search fallback or benchmark-shaped order-specific tables were added.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune the landed high-p 12/14/15/18 MOLR-from-MOLS lane from the ideas backlog and shift attention to order-20 high-p rows, especially 20-5 and 20-7..20, where broader RTD/MOLR/RGDD theory is still missing.

description: Tried extending `molr_from_mols` to theorem-backed direct-product MOLS banks so order-20 high-p rows could reuse the same Sharma-Das truncation route. name: Solver6 runtime-quality frontier at 50 people / 20 weeks

description: Baseline run for the new solver6 autoresearch target at week_cap=20 and max_people=50. The quality-first objective cost starts at 192188052267 with 1452 linear hits out of 1600 eligible week-runs, and runtime is dominated by exact-block-only seed construction (about 4.98M of 5.88M ms).

asi.hypothesis: The expanded 50-person solver6 benchmark remains seed-dominated, so the best first optimization lane should be exact-block relabeling/seed construction rather than local-search scans.
asi.learned: Baseline confirms the prior smaller-scope attribution scaled up: exact_block_only cases account for the overwhelming majority of runtime, heuristic tails are a distant second, and scan time is tiny relative to total wall time. Quality baseline is 1452/1600 linear hits with 4392 total linear gap and no timeouts/errors.
asi.next_action_hint: Profile and simplify backend/core/src/solver6/seed/relabeling.rs first, especially repeated full seed rebuild + full pair rescoring inside greedily_improve_copy_permutation.

description: Reworked greedy exact-block relabeling to keep an incremental pair-frequency state across copy-permutation swaps instead of rebuilding and rescoring the full composed seed for every candidate. Benchmark quality stayed identical, but total runtime collapsed from 5.88M ms to 127.7k ms and exact_block_only runtime fell from about 4.98M ms to 70.1k ms on the 50-person scope.

asi.hypothesis: Exact-block relabeling was paying an enormous avoidable full-recompute cost; evaluating copy swaps directly against an incremental pair state should preserve quality while cutting the dominant seed bottleneck dramatically.
asi.learned: That was the main bottleneck. The incremental copy-swap evaluator preserved all benchmark-quality metrics exactly while delivering a ~46x total runtime speedup and ~71x exact_block_only seed runtime speedup on the expanded scope. Search telemetry stayed essentially unchanged, confirming the gain came from relabeling/seed construction rather than local search.
asi.next_action_hint: Now that the seed bottleneck is mostly gone, inspect the remaining runtime concentration between heuristic tails and the benchmark/report harness itself, and consider adding finer seed-build phase timings so future runs can target the next bottleneck precisely.

description: Added an early exit in greedy exact-block relabeling when the current seed already reaches the known linear optimum for the active objective. Benchmark quality stayed identical again, and runtime dropped further from 127.7k ms to 119.8k ms by avoiding useless post-optimum swap scans in many exact-block prefixes.

asi.hypothesis: Many exact-block relabeling seeds already reach the linear lower bound before the greedy loop exhausts all remaining copy passes, so stopping immediately at the known optimum should save time without changing output.
asi.learned: That shortcut is valid under the current strict objective because once linear mode reaches the lower bound, no later equal-score/equal-gap move would have been accepted anyway. The improvement is modest but real: exact_block_only runtime dropped another ~7s and requested-tail prefixes also got cheaper, while all quality metrics remained identical.
asi.next_action_hint: The remaining runtime is now roughly split between exact_block_only and heuristic_tail. Next inspect heuristic tail construction and consider whether per-week candidate scoring or pair-state updates there can be made more incremental or more selective.

description: Collapsed heuristic-tail candidate scoring to a closed-form pair-addition delta so each candidate addition does one pair-count lookup instead of repeated score-delta queries. Quality stayed identical and heuristic-tail time moved slightly, but the overall objective ended marginally worse than the current best, so treat this as noise and roll it back.

asi.hypothesis: Heuristic tail building was doing redundant per-pair delta work, so closed-form deltas might trim the remaining heuristic-tail runtime without affecting schedule quality.
asi.learned: The optimization is probably directionally fine but too small relative to current noise on the full benchmark. It slightly improved heuristic_tail_runtime_ms but regressed exact_block_only_runtime_ms in the same run, leaving total_runtime_ms 142ms worse than the current best. This lane does not yet show a reliable whole-benchmark win.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary objective was slightly worse than the current best, and the effect size is tiny enough to treat as noise rather than a clear keep.
asi.next_action_hint: Either rerun this exact change to check if it is noise-positive, or pivot to a higher-leverage remaining bottleneck such as heuristic-tail construction strategy or benchmark/report overhead instrumentation.

description: Changed relabeling pair-adjustment aggregation from incremental linear-search merging to collect-then-sort compaction, hoping the larger multiweek swap adjustments would benefit from cheaper inserts. Quality stayed identical, but runtime regressed sharply across both exact-block and heuristic-tail families, so this aggregation shape is worse in practice here.

asi.hypothesis: Relabeling swap adjustments were large enough that deferring merge work to a final sort/compact pass would beat repeated linear scans during accumulation.
asi.learned: That hypothesis was wrong for the current workload. The collect-then-sort path added significant overhead and made the full benchmark about 49s slower, with exact_block_only and heuristic_tail both regressing badly. The prior small-vector incremental merge is better for current solver6 relabeling workloads.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary objective regressed materially with no quality gain.
asi.next_action_hint: Avoid sort-heavy adjustment aggregation in relabeling. If optimizing this path further, look at cheaper direct indexing / fewer Result-bound checks instead of changing the aggregation strategy.

description: Changed mixed-tail seed selection to break linear-score ties with squared repeat excess before max pair frequency. Linear outcomes stayed identical, but the selected family mix shifted away from requested tails and reduced total squared gap by 1986 with only a 284ms runtime cost, improving the quality-first benchmark objective.

asi.hypothesis: When linear-repeat scores tie, explicit squared-repeat tie-breaking should align mixed-seed selection better with the benchmark than using max pair frequency alone.
asi.learned: This was a real quality improvement. The benchmark kept exactly the same linear hit/miss profile and runtime stayed essentially flat, but mixed-seed choice changed materially (requested_tail_atom 195->168, heuristic_tail 164->193) and squared_repeat gap improved by 1986. Max pair frequency was only a proxy; explicit squared damage works better for this benchmark.
asi.next_action_hint: Look for other places where solver6 still uses proxy tie-breaks instead of explicit benchmark-relevant damage measures, especially in mixed-seed ranking and local-search move ordering for linear-optimal states.

description: Tried adding squared-repeat tie-breaking to same-week swap selection so linear-mode hill climbing would prefer lower squared damage among equal linear moves. It improved squared metrics and even shaved total linear gap slightly, but it also lost one linear hit (1452->1451), which is fatal under the quality-first objective, so this move-ordering change is not acceptable as-is.

asi.hypothesis: When active search mode is linear, using squared repeat excess as a secondary tie-break among equal-linear swap moves might improve final schedules without changing the primary linear result.
asi.learned: The tie-break is strong enough to change search trajectories materially: squared_hit_count improved 924->929 and squared_gap_sum improved, but one previously linear-hit case fell to miss, which dominates everything else in the benchmark objective. So search tie-breaks are much riskier than mixed-seed tie-breaks.
asi.rollback_reason: Primary objective regressed because linear_hit_count dropped by one and linear_miss_count increased by one.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep squared-aware tie-breaking in seed selection, but avoid applying it blindly inside local search. If revisiting this lane, gate it behind a proof that the linear status cannot worsen, or only use it after linear optimality is already certified.

description: Stopped rebuilding the same exact-block prefix seed separately for requested-tail and heuristic-tail candidates, reusing a shared prefix seed during mixed-tail evaluation. Quality stayed identical to the new squared-aware seed-selection baseline, and total runtime dropped from 120.1k ms to 116.1k ms with most of the win coming from lower heuristic-tail build cost.

asi.hypothesis: Non-divisible mixed-tail cases were redundantly paying for the same greedy exact-block prefix construction multiple times, so sharing that prefix seed across candidate families should reduce runtime without affecting quality.
asi.learned: That redundancy was real. Reusing the prefix seed preserved all linear and squared quality metrics exactly and cut another ~4.0s off total runtime, mostly by reducing heuristic_tail runtime while leaving exact_block_only behavior unchanged.
asi.next_action_hint: The remaining hot runtime is still split between exact_block_only and heuristic_tail. Next look for other duplicated work in mixed-tail generation (especially dominant-prefix rebuilds or repeated telemetry recomputation) or additional safe quality-aligned tie-break improvements in seed ranking.

description: Stopped recomputing solver6 reporting metrics from raw schedules when the search/seed pipeline already had equivalent telemetry in memory. Reporting now reuses selected-seed telemetry and best pair-frequency state from local search, preserving the exact same benchmark outputs while reducing total runtime from 116.1k ms to 114.7k ms.

asi.hypothesis: The frontier benchmark was still rescoring many schedules inside reporting even though solver6 already had equivalent pair-frequency telemetry available, so reusing that state should reduce runtime without affecting results.
asi.learned: That reporting recompute overhead was real but moderate. Reusing seed telemetry and best pair-state preserved all linear and squared metrics exactly and shaved another ~1.36s off the full 50-person benchmark, mostly from lower heuristic-tail/report overhead rather than search changes.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue looking for safe state-reuse wins in the benchmark path, especially around any remaining schedule-to-summary recomputations or duplicated work in dominant-prefix mixed-tail generation.

description: Reused incremental pair-frequency state to build requested-tail and heuristic-tail seed telemetry instead of rescoring their full schedules from scratch. Quality stayed identical to the current best, and runtime improved again from 114.7k ms to 112.4k ms, mostly by shaving remaining mixed-tail overhead.

asi.hypothesis: Mixed-tail seed construction still paid avoidable full-schedule telemetry recomputes after it had already built equivalent pair-frequency state, so reusing that state should reduce runtime without changing outputs.
asi.learned: That leftover telemetry recompute cost was real. Reusing pair state for heuristic and requested-tail telemetry preserved all benchmark metrics exactly and cut another ~2.36s from the full run, mainly in heuristic/requested-tail runtime.
asi.next_action_hint: The next safe runtime lane is probably more internal state reuse in mixed-tail or relabeling rather than search-policy changes, since seed/report reuse keeps paying while search tie-break changes have already shown quality risk.

description: Tried carrying one shared prefix PairFrequencyState across requested-tail and heuristic-tail candidate builds, instead of rebuilding it separately for each candidate. Quality stayed identical, but whole-benchmark runtime regressed slightly versus the current best, so this extra context wrapper does not pay for itself.

asi.hypothesis: Reusing one reconstructed prefix pair state across multiple mixed-tail candidates would save enough repeated raw-schedule pair-state rebuilding to reduce tail runtime without affecting outputs.
asi.rollback_reason: The measured whole-benchmark objective regressed slightly: runtime rose from the current best 112.4k ms to 113.7k ms with identical quality metrics, so the added wrapper/state plumbing did not produce a reliable net win.
asi.learned: At current scale, one shared prefix pair-state reconstruction is not a worthwhile optimization by itself. The remaining cost is probably deeper inside heuristic-tail generation or exact-block relabeling rather than this small setup overhead.
asi.next_action_hint: Look for optimizations that remove more substantial work inside heuristic-tail week construction or relabeling candidate evaluation, or add instrumentation that isolates those costs more directly.

description: Replaced heuristic-tail candidate scoring's generic pair-penalty delta calls with direct closed-form increment math for linear, triangular, and squared repeat penalties. Outputs stayed identical, but the whole benchmark still regressed versus the current best, so this local micro-optimization is not paying off end to end.

asi.hypothesis: Heuristic-tail construction spends enough time in per-candidate score_delta_for_pair_change calls that replacing them with direct increment formulas should reduce mixed-tail runtime without changing schedules.
asi.rollback_reason: The benchmark got slightly worse overall despite identical quality. The local scoring shortcut did not translate into a net runtime win on the full 50-person benchmark.
asi.learned: This part of heuristic-tail scoring is not the dominant remaining cost, or the savings are below the current benchmark noise floor. More structural work is needed than small inner-loop algebra substitutions.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus next on larger-grain duplicated work or on measuring exact subphase runtime, especially inside heuristic-tail week construction and exact-block relabeling.

description: Reused the final greedy relabeling PairFrequencyState when building exact-block seeds, so the post-search seed assembly no longer rescored the composed schedule from scratch. Quality stayed identical and exact-block-only runtime moved in the right direction, but the whole-benchmark objective was still slightly worse than the current best, so this was not a reliable net win.

asi.hypothesis: Exact-block seed construction still paid an avoidable full schedule rescore after greedy relabeling already had the final pair state, so reusing that state for seed telemetry should reduce exact_block_only runtime without changing outputs.
asi.rollback_reason: The whole-benchmark metric was still slightly worse than the current best (112.736s vs 112.387s total runtime) even though exact_block_only runtime improved somewhat, so this change did not produce a trustworthy end-to-end win.
asi.learned: The post-search telemetry rescore in exact-block seed assembly is real but not large enough by itself. Remaining exact_block_only cost is still dominated by the relabeling search itself, not final seed packaging.
asi.next_action_hint: Look for wins inside relabeling candidate evaluation or scan ordering/pruning, or add cheap timing instrumentation that isolates where exact-block-only time is still going.

description: Added an early-return prune in greedy relabeling swap scans: if the current scan encounters the first improving move that already reaches the known objective optimum, stop scanning the rest of that neighborhood. The selected move remains deterministic and matched exhaustive selection in tests, but the full 50-person benchmark still regressed slightly versus the current best.

asi.hypothesis: Many relabeling scans still waste time finishing a full neighborhood even after they have already found the lexicographically earliest move that reaches the known optimum for the current objective, so short-circuiting those scans should reduce exact-block runtime without changing outputs.
asi.rollback_reason: The whole-benchmark runtime regressed slightly despite identical quality, so this prune is either too rare on the 50-person frontier or too small to clear benchmark noise reliably.
asi.learned: Greedy relabeling rarely spends meaningful time after finding a globally optimal single swap, at least on the current benchmark distribution. Remaining relabeling cost is more about evaluating lots of non-optimal candidates than finishing scans after optimal candidates appear.
asi.next_action_hint: Pursue broader relabeling-side pruning or cached per-copy summaries that reduce the cost of evaluating many candidate swaps, rather than only terminating a few lucky scans earlier.

description: Tried caching every pair index inside PairUniverse so hot paths could replace arithmetic index computation with table lookups. The change was fully semantic-preserving, but it materially regressed the 50-person benchmark, likely because the larger universe state and extra memory traffic outweighed the saved arithmetic.

asi.hypothesis: PairUniverse::pair_index is called pervasively in relabeling, tail building, and search, so replacing repeated triangular-index arithmetic with precomputed table lookups might produce a broad runtime win without affecting outputs.
asi.rollback_reason: The benchmark regressed significantly, from the current best 112.4k ms to 118.8k ms, with exact-block and heuristic-tail runtimes both getting worse. The table lookup added more cost than it removed.
asi.learned: For these small n<=50 pure-SGP instances, pair-index arithmetic is cheaper than chasing a precomputed matrix. Broad caching at this level is the wrong direction.
asi.next_action_hint: Avoid more memory-heavy low-level caching. Focus instead on pruning candidate evaluations or on higher-level relabeling/tail-assembly work reduction.

description: Pruned relabeling swap scans for source-person pairs that are weekwise equivalent inside the exact atom, since swapping such sources is a no-op under any permutation. The pruning was semantically safe and tested, but it did not improve the full 50-person benchmark and regressed slightly versus the current best.

asi.hypothesis: Some exact-block atoms may contain source-person symmetries, so skipping swaps between weekwise-equivalent source signatures could reduce relabeling candidate scans without changing outcomes.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark still regressed slightly versus the current best, so either these exact equivalences are too rare on the current frontier or the extra context bookkeeping outweighed the skipped evaluations.
asi.learned: Simple exact-signature symmetry pruning is not a meaningful win on this benchmark. Remaining relabeling cost likely needs stronger pruning than just zero-effect source swaps.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus next on broader non-competitive swap pruning, mixed-seed candidate pruning, or instrumentation that exposes where relabeling still spends most of its time.

description: Skipped building the dominant-prefix-tail mixed-seed family whenever an exact requested-tail atom exists. This produced a massive runtime win by eliminating almost all dominant-prefix work, but it lost one squared hit and slightly worsened squared gap, so it fails the quality-first objective despite preserving all linear metrics.

asi.hypothesis: Because dominant_prefix_tail is almost never selected when an exact requested tail exists, skipping that family in those cases could preserve quality while removing a large chunk of mixed-seed runtime.
asi.rollback_reason: The benchmark lost one squared hit (924 -> 923) and slightly worsened squared gap, so the quality-first objective got worse even though runtime dropped dramatically to 78.9s.
asi.learned: Dominant-prefix-tail is rare but still quality-relevant in at least one requested-tail-supported case. It cannot be removed wholesale under the current objective. However, this experiment shows that mixed-seed family pruning has very high runtime leverage if a more selective, quality-preserving criterion can be found.
asi.next_action_hint: Try to identify a safer dominance test for skipping dominant-prefix-tail only when another family already dominates it on the benchmark-relevant tuple, or focus on reducing dominant-prefix-tail build cost rather than removing it entirely.

description: Warm-started dominant-prefix-tail relabeling from the already-optimized prefix relabeling plan plus one fresh identity copy, instead of recomputing the larger exact-block composition from scratch. Quality stayed identical and total runtime improved from 112.4k ms to 107.1k ms, with most of the win coming from lower heuristic-tail/related mixed-seed runtime.

asi.hypothesis: Dominant-prefix-tail construction was paying to solve the ceil-copy exact-block relabeling problem from scratch even though mixed-tail selection had already optimized the prefix copies, so warm-starting from the prefix plan should preserve quality while cutting mixed-seed runtime.
asi.learned: This warm start is a real win. It preserved all linear and squared quality metrics exactly while reducing total runtime by about 5.3s. The dominant-prefix family still needs to exist for quality, but it does not need a cold-start relabeling search.
asi.next_action_hint: Next target the remaining mixed-tail cost with selective, quality-preserving candidate pruning or further warm-start/state reuse, especially where requested-tail and heuristic-tail still rebuild more than necessary.

description: Changed exact-block relabeling to build copy permutations incrementally: solve one exact copy first, then warm-start each additional copy from the previously optimized prefix plus one fresh identity copy. Runtime rose, but linear quality improved materially on the 50-person frontier: 1452 -> 1455 linear hits and linear gap fell from 4392 -> 4318, which is a strong keep under the quality-first objective.

asi.hypothesis: The same prefix warm-start that sped up dominant-prefix-tail construction might also lead greedy exact-block relabeling into better local optima if full-copy plans are built incrementally instead of starting every horizon from all-identity permutations.
asi.learned: That hypothesis was correct for quality. Incremental copy-by-copy warm starts improved the benchmark's primary objective substantially by gaining 3 linear hits and reducing linear gap by 74, even though exact-block runtime increased. This is a quality win first and foremost.
asi.tradeoff: Squared gap and total runtime worsened, but the lexicographic objective improved because linear misses and linear gap dominate those secondary costs.
asi.next_action_hint: Now that incremental relabeling warm starts improved quality, the next safe lane is to recover runtime inside this new path—e.g. avoid rebuilding full context from scratch for every intermediate copy count, or add shared-context warm starts that preserve the new quality profile.

description: Recovered some runtime from the new incremental exact-block relabeling path by reusing one exact-block composition context across intermediate copy counts, instead of rebuilding the context from fresh input clones on every warm-start step. The improved linear quality profile stayed identical, and total runtime dropped from 133.9k ms to 133.0k ms.

asi.hypothesis: After switching exact-block relabeling to incremental copy-by-copy warm starts, some of the runtime regression came from rebuilding exact-block contexts and cloned inputs for each intermediate copy count rather than from the better search path itself.
asi.learned: That overhead was real but modest. Reusing the base context across incremental copy counts preserved the improved 1455-hit linear profile exactly and recovered about 0.95s of runtime, mainly inside exact_block_only.
asi.next_action_hint: The next likely safe lane is to keep the new incremental relabeling quality path but attack its remaining exact_block_only cost more directly—e.g. shared state/context reuse across intermediate copy counts or other warm-start-preserving reductions.

description: Tried carrying the incremental exact-block warm-start search as a live GreedyRelabelingState, extending its pair state with one identity copy instead of rebuilding a fresh state from the warm-start plan at each copy count. The quality profile stayed identical, but runtime regressed slightly versus the current best, so the extra stateful path is not paying off.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining runtime in the incremental exact-block relabeling path comes partly from rebuilding a fresh pair-frequency state from the warm-start plan at each intermediate copy count, so extending the previous state directly should recover more runtime without changing the improved 1455-hit linear profile.
asi.rollback_reason: Whole-benchmark runtime regressed from the current best 132.994s to 134.816s with identical quality, so direct live-state extension did not beat the simpler context-reuse warm-start path.
asi.learned: The expensive part is not just rebuilding the pair state for each intermediate copy count, or the extra stateful bookkeeping outweighed that saving. The current simpler warm-start implementation remains preferable.
asi.next_action_hint: Look next at higher-level pruning or dominance tests in mixed-seed selection, or at more targeted context/state reuse inside exact_block_only that does not complicate the incremental path as much.

description: Stopped rebuilding full temporary schedules when initializing greedy relabeling state from a plan. The relabeling path now constructs PairFrequencyState directly from the atom schedule plus copy permutations, preserving the improved 1455-hit linear profile while cutting total runtime from 133.0k ms to 132.1k ms.

asi.hypothesis: A noticeable part of the remaining exact-block runtime comes from building temporary composed schedules purely to initialize greedy relabeling pair state, so constructing the pair state directly from atom weeks and copy permutations should recover runtime without changing the new higher-quality relabeling behavior.
asi.learned: That temporary schedule construction was real overhead. Direct pair-state construction preserved the improved linear profile exactly and recovered about 0.9s of runtime, mainly from exact_block_only and heuristic-tail paths that initialize relabeling state.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep attacking exact-block initialization overhead or mixed-tail candidate construction costs, but preserve the new incremental relabeling quality gains rather than reverting to the older faster-but-weaker path.

description: Tried building exact-block seed telemetry incrementally while materializing the final schedule, so packaging no longer rebuilt pair telemetry from the finished schedule. Quality stayed identical, but total runtime regressed from 132.1k ms to 134.9k ms, so this extra bookkeeping is not worth keeping.

asi.hypothesis: Exact-block seed packaging still spends meaningful time rebuilding pair telemetry from the completed schedule, so updating PairFrequencyState inline during schedule materialization should preserve quality and reduce exact_block_only overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: Inline pair-state maintenance during final seed packaging added more overhead than it removed; exact_block_only and heuristic-tail runtime both increased while quality stayed flat.
asi.next_action_hint: Avoid more micro-optimizations in final seed packaging for now. Focus instead on higher-level relabeling pruning/state reuse or honest mixed-seed dominance tests.
asi.learned: The expensive part is not the final telemetry recomputation alone. Updating every emitted block's pair counts during seed assembly regressed wall time even though it removed a later from_schedule pass.

description: Replaced hot relabeling-path Result-heavy permutation/pair-index lookups with direct indexed access plus a fast arithmetic pair-index helper. The improved 1455-hit / 4318-gap quality profile stayed identical, and total runtime fell from 132.1k ms to 128.4k ms, mostly from exact_block_only and heuristic-tail relabeling work.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining exact-block relabeling runtime is still dominated by millions of tiny hot-loop permutation and pair-index lookups, so removing Result/error-construction overhead in the validated inner loops should recover real time without changing search behavior.
asi.learned: That hot-loop overhead was material. Direct indexed permutation access plus a fast arithmetic pair-index helper preserved the stronger linear profile exactly and cut about 3.6s of runtime, with most of the win showing up in exact_block_only and heuristic-tail seed construction.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep looking for cheap work removal inside relabeling evaluation and honest mixed-seed dominance tests, but deprioritize seed-packaging micro-optimizations that already regressed.

description: Stopped materializing and rescoring the extra trailing weeks of dominant-prefix-tail candidates just to truncate them away. The relabeling search still optimizes against the full ceil-div exact block, but seed construction now emits only the requested prefix, preserving the 1455-hit / 4318-gap quality profile while cutting runtime from 128.4k ms to 125.7k ms.

asi.hypothesis: Dominant-prefix-tail candidates waste real time by fully materializing the extra ceil-div copy and then truncating/retelemetering it, so emitting only the requested prefix of the optimized plan should preserve behavior while shaving mixed-tail overhead.
asi.learned: That truncation path was real overhead. Keeping the larger warm-start relabeling optimization but constructing only the requested prefix reduced total runtime by another ~2.7s with identical quality, mostly through lower exact_block_only-adjacent and heuristic-tail/mixed-seed costs.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue removing honest wasted work around mixed-tail candidate construction and relabeling materialization, especially cases where full schedules are built only to discard part of them later.

description: Tried extending the hot-loop fast pair-index arithmetic into mixed-tail heuristic scoring and skipping duplicate active/linear delta calls when the active model is already linear. Quality stayed identical, but total runtime regressed from 125.7k ms to 128.0k ms, so this mixed-tail micro-optimization is not worth keeping.

asi.hypothesis: Heuristic-tail construction still spends enough time in pair-index/delta hot loops that fast arithmetic pair indexing and avoiding duplicate active-vs-linear delta calls should reduce mixed-tail runtime without changing behavior.
asi.rollback_reason: The extra mixed-tail micro-optimization regressed wall time overall even though heuristic_tail_runtime_ms improved slightly; exact_block_only cost rose enough that the total benchmark got worse.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not chase more local mixed-tail arithmetic tweaks for now. Prefer broader wasted-work removal or exact-block relabeling reductions that move the total runtime directly.
asi.learned: Small heuristic-tail hot-loop tweaks can look promising in the family-local metric while still worsening the global benchmark. The current bottleneck remains dominated by exact-block relabeling rather than heuristic-tail arithmetic alone.

description: Tried deferring dominant-prefix-tail seed materialization by selecting from cheap prefix telemetry first and only building the full schedule if that candidate won. Quality stayed identical, but total runtime regressed from 125.7k ms to 127.2k ms, so the extra telemetry-only path did not beat the simpler direct build.

asi.hypothesis: Dominant-prefix-tail candidates are almost never selected, so computing only their prefix telemetry during selection and materializing the full seed only on demand should cut mixed-seed overhead without changing outcomes.
asi.rollback_reason: The new telemetry-only preparation path still paid substantial pair-state work and ended up slower overall than directly building the dominant candidate under the current implementation.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not chase more two-path dominant candidate preparation right now. Prefer simpler wasted-work removal or relabeling candidate-evaluation reductions inside the exact-block hot path.
asi.learned: The dominant candidate's schedule materialization was not the whole story. Replacing it with a separate telemetry-only path added enough duplicate work to lose about 1.4s overall even though selection semantics stayed identical.

description: Precomputed exact-block same-group membership flags so relabeling swap evaluation no longer linearly scans each block's mate list just to detect the no-op same-block case. The stronger 1455-hit / 4318-gap profile stayed identical, and total runtime fell from 125.7k ms to 124.6k ms, with gains showing up in both exact-block-only and heuristic-tail paths.

asi.hypothesis: Relabeling swap evaluation still spends meaningful time doing tiny linear `contains` scans on per-week mate lists, so precomputing same-group membership flags should remove hot-loop work without affecting the search trajectory.
asi.learned: Those repeated same-block membership checks were material enough to matter. Adding a compact boolean membership view preserved behavior exactly and shaved about 1.1s from the benchmark, mostly by lowering exact-block relabeling cost and mixed-tail cases that depend on the same machinery.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep targeting cheap repeated work inside exact-block swap evaluation, especially hot-loop predicates and small repeated lookups, but avoid more complex two-path candidate preparation that already regressed.

description: Moved relabeling score-delta accumulation onto a trusted fast path that reads pair counts directly instead of rebuilding Result/error plumbing for every already-validated +/-1 adjustment. Quality stayed identical at the stronger 1455-hit / 4318-gap profile, and total runtime dropped dramatically from 124.6k ms to 107.4k ms, with the biggest win in exact_block_only relabeling.

asi.hypothesis: After removing lookup overhead, a large remaining exact-block cost is still Result-heavy per-adjustment scoring in relabeling, so a trusted fast path for already-validated pair deltas should recover substantial runtime without changing semantics.
asi.learned: That per-adjustment validation overhead was much larger than it looked. The fast score-delta path preserved exact behavior and cut about 17.3s from the benchmark, mostly from exact-block-only relabeling and secondarily from heuristic-tail cases that share the same machinery.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue attacking exact-block relabeling hot loops with validated fast paths for already-proven-safe internal operations, while avoiding more speculative mixed-tail-only micro-optimizations and two-path candidate-preparation schemes.

description: Extended the trusted fast-path idea from relabeling score-delta accumulation into direct pair-state application for already-validated internal updates. Quality stayed identical, but total runtime regressed from 107.4k ms to 108.1k ms, so the extra inlined update path is not worth keeping.

asi.hypothesis: If Result-heavy validated pair-state updates are also a meaningful residual cost, then a trusted fast apply path for internal +/-1 relabeling updates should compound the earlier fast score-delta win.
asi.rollback_reason: The broader fast-apply path added code and duplicated bookkeeping without beating the current optimizer/codegen balance; runtime regressed slightly overall.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the fast score-delta path, but stop short of cloning the full pair-state mutation logic into a second trusted path. Focus next on cheaper predicates or other exact-block relabeling hot-loop work instead.
asi.learned: The relabeling hot path benefits strongly from cutting per-adjustment validation in read-only scoring, but not every corresponding write-side fast path pays off. The win was specifically in score accumulation, not a blanket duplication of pair-state mutation logic.

description: Replaced the per-swap small-vector adjustment accumulator with a reusable dense scratch accumulator keyed by pair index. Relabeling now reuses one touched-pair workspace across swap scans and only materializes pair adjustments for the incumbent best move, preserving the 1455-hit / 4318-gap quality profile while cutting runtime from 107.4k ms to 81.8k ms.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining exact-block relabeling bottleneck is the repeated per-swap adjustment accumulation itself, especially linear searches and allocations in the small-vector merger, so a reusable dense scratch accumulator should remove substantial candidate-evaluation overhead without changing semantics.
asi.learned: That accumulation overhead was extremely large. Reusing a dense touched-pair scratch structure and materializing adjustments only for winning candidates preserved behavior exactly and cut about 25.5s from the benchmark, with major wins in exact_block_only and heuristic_tail paths.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep targeting relabeling candidate-evaluation overhead and other repeated per-swap scratch work; this lane is still highly productive, but avoid regressed ideas like duplicate write-side fast paths or telemetry-only dominant-candidate preparation.

description: Reused the dense relabeling scratch accumulator across repeated best-swap scans for a copy permutation instead of reallocating the workspace on every improvement step. Quality stayed identical at the stronger 1455-hit / 4318-gap profile, and runtime improved again from 81.8k ms to 79.5k ms.

asi.hypothesis: After landing the dense touched-pair scratch accumulator, its own repeated allocation across successive best-swap scans is still avoidable overhead, so reusing one workspace per copy-improvement pass should cut runtime further without changing behavior.
asi.learned: That scratch allocation churn was still measurable. Reusing the same dense accumulator across repeated find-best iterations preserved behavior exactly and shaved another ~2.4s from the benchmark, mainly from exact-block-only relabeling.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep pushing on relabeling hot-loop scratch reuse and low-level repeated-work removal. The exact-block relabeling path is still the most productive optimization lane by far.

description: Tried reusing the incumbent best-adjustment vector in place so scratch materialization no longer allocates a fresh Vec each time a better swap appears during scanning. Quality stayed identical, but total runtime regressed slightly from 79.5k ms to 80.8k ms, so the extra mutation path is not worth keeping.

asi.hypothesis: Best-swap replacement still allocates and copies many adjustment vectors during relabeling scans, so reusing the incumbent Vec buffer in place should shave more hot-loop overhead without changing behavior.
asi.rollback_reason: The in-place incumbent-update path added enough branching and mutation overhead that it lost to the simpler allocate-on-replacement approach.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the dense scratch accumulator and its per-copy reuse, but avoid further incumbent-bookkeeping complexity unless profiling shows repeated Vec allocation is dominant.
asi.learned: The big relabeling win came from replacing per-swap accumulation and scratch allocation, not from micromanaging the occasional incumbent replacement buffer.

description: Stopped clearing dense relabeling scratch state pair-by-pair between swap evaluations by switching the touched-pair workspace to generation stamps. The stronger 1455-hit / 4318-gap quality profile stayed identical, and total runtime dropped again from 79.5k ms to 73.5k ms.

asi.hypothesis: Even after dense scratch accumulation landed, clearing touched-pair flags/deltas on every candidate still costs real time, so generation-stamped scratch reuse should eliminate that reset overhead without changing semantics.
asi.learned: That reset work was still substantial. Generation-stamped scratch reuse preserved exact behavior and saved about 6.0s overall, with large gains in exact-block-only relabeling and a smaller follow-on win in heuristic-tail/mixed-seed runtime.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep targeting relabeling scratch and per-candidate bookkeeping overhead; this exact-block hot-loop lane is still paying off strongly, while incumbent-buffer micromanagement and write-side fast-path duplication are not.

description: Precomputed the source-mate contributions for each relabeling swap pair, so hot swap evaluation now iterates flat cached mate lists instead of looping week-by-week through groupmate flags and per-week vectors. The stronger 1455-hit / 4318-gap quality profile stayed identical, and total runtime dropped again from 73.5k ms to 71.4k ms.

asi.hypothesis: Even after dense scratch generation stamps landed, relabeling still spends time repeatedly walking per-week groupmate structures for every swap candidate; caching each source-pair's affected mate lists once per exact-block context should reduce that candidate-evaluation overhead without changing semantics.
asi.learned: That higher-level relabeling summary cache paid off. Flattening swap effects into per-source-pair mate lists preserved exact behavior and shaved another ~2.1s off the benchmark, with the gain concentrated in exact-block-only relabeling and a smaller heuristic-tail follow-on win.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue attacking hot relabeling candidate-evaluation overhead with cached structural summaries or other honest reuse, but prune stale low-level buffer-micromanagement ideas that already regressed.

description: Tried maintaining relabeling score deltas incrementally inside the dense scratch workspace so candidate evaluation could avoid the final touched-pair scoring pass. Quality stayed identical, but runtime regressed sharply from 71.4k ms to 93.7k ms, so the extra per-apply bookkeeping is not worth it.

asi.hypothesis: If the dense scratch tracks active and linear score deltas incrementally during each pair adjustment, swap evaluation can avoid a second pass over touched pairs and shave more time off the hot relabeling loop.
asi.rollback_reason: Inline score-delta maintenance added too much work to every scratch apply and overwhelmed the saved touched-pair fold, causing a large runtime regression despite unchanged quality.
asi.learned: The hot relabeling path prefers cheap write-side accumulation plus a separate read-side fold over touched pairs. Moving more logic into each apply call repeats too much penalty bookkeeping and is substantially worse than the current generation-stamped scratch + final fold design.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune incremental per-apply score-delta bookkeeping from the active ideas and keep focusing on structural relabeling summaries or cheaper cached lookups that reduce candidate work without making each scratch update heavier.

description: Tried deferring swap-adjustment materialization until after the best relabeling candidate was selected, then reevaluating only the winner once to build its pair adjustments. Quality stayed identical, but runtime regressed slightly from 71.4k ms to 72.6k ms, so the extra winner reevaluation costs more than the avoided intermediate materializations.

asi.hypothesis: If relabeling scans stop materializing pair adjustments for every improving incumbent and only reevaluate the final winning swap once, candidate selection should get cheaper without changing semantics.
asi.rollback_reason: The extra reevaluation of the winning candidate outweighed the savings from skipping interim materializations, producing a small but likely real runtime regression.
asi.learned: Materializing adjustments only for improving incumbents is already cheap enough after the dense scratch changes. Replaying the winning candidate once at the end is not a net win.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune winner-reevaluation materialization deferral from the active relabeling ideas and keep targeting structural candidate-evaluation reductions rather than extra end-of-scan passes.

name: Solver6 frontier benchmark probe at 100 people / 20 weeks

description: Ran a user-requested 100-person frontier probe by temporarily raising the benchmark limit from 50 to 100. The run completed successfully, but it is a workload probe rather than a code improvement: objective_cost=9550238650903, total_runtime_ms=1425503 (~23.8 min), with 2243/2680 successful week-runs and 87 timeouts; heuristic-tail work dominated runtime at 861835 ms.

asi.hypothesis: Recent relabeling/runtime improvements might make the 100-person / 20-week frontier sweep viable enough to benchmark directly.
asi.rollback_reason: No solver change was being evaluated here; this was a temporary benchmark-scope probe requested by the user, so the 100-person script override should not replace the normal 50-person autoresearch target.
asi.learned: The 100-person sweep is now runnable, but it is still expensive and no longer timeout-free. Exact-block-only work took 554248 ms, heuristic-tail took 861835 ms, and 87 week-runs still timed out under the current 2-second per-cell limit.
asi.next_action_hint: Restore the standard 50-person autoresearch scope after reporting these results. If we later switch to a real 100-person optimization loop, start a fresh workload-specific experiment track and focus first on the heavy heuristic-tail and exact-block seed costs revealed by this probe.

description: Baseline for the reinitialized solver6 100-person frontier target with 20 weeks and 4 benchmark workers. This establishes the new quality-first objective on the larger workload before further solver6 relabeling and mixed-tail optimization work.

asi.hypothesis: Reinitialize the solver6 autoresearch target from 50 people to 100 people while using 4 benchmark workers; first establish the honest baseline for the larger workload.
asi.target_change: max_people 50 -> 100 with benchmark parallel_jobs fixed at 4
asi.key_learning: The 100-person frontier remains quality-limited and expensive: 94 timeouts, 343 linear misses, and heuristic-tail runtime now dominates exact-block runtime.
asi.bottleneck_signal: heuristic_tail_runtime_ms 952634 exceeds exact_block_only_runtime_ms 683979; scan_time_ms is also material at 315230.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus first on honest runtime reductions in heuristic-tail and exact-block relabeling candidate evaluation without sacrificing the observed 2243 linear hits.

description: Fast-path heuristic-tail candidate scoring by using known-valid pair indexing/count lookups and avoiding duplicate linear-delta work when the active model is already linear. Wall-clock and total runtime nudged down slightly, but benchmark quality regressed on the 100-person target.

asi.hypothesis: Result-heavy heuristic-tail candidate scoring overhead might be large enough at 100 people that replacing it with known-valid pair-index/count fast paths would improve runtime without changing choices.
asi.rollback_reason: The optimization was not behavior-preserving in practice on the canonical 100-person benchmark: linear_gap_sum worsened from 50170 to 50213 and squared_miss_count rose from 956 to 957, making the primary metric worse despite slightly lower runtime.
asi.quality_regression_signal: Same timeout count and linear hit count, but worse linear gap and squared miss/gap totals indicate changed heuristic-tail candidate tie behavior or ordering sensitivity.
asi.next_action_hint: Prefer optimizations that preserve heuristic-tail decision ordering exactly, or target exact-block relabeling / reporting-side instrumentation before touching heuristic-tail candidate ranking internals.
